# 3. Methodology

## 3.1 Overview

This section describes the three-component methodology employed to surface and validate implicit user preferences in collaborative writing systems. We define an *implicit preference* as a latent disposition that consistently and systematically influences a user's editing behavior but that the user does not voluntarily or reliably articulate when prompted directly. Our approach proceeds in three stages: (1) the design of a large language model (LLM) elicitation pipeline that generates candidate preference representations from behavioral traces; (2) the collection and annotation of behavioral data from writers interacting with the system; and (3) the evaluation of elicited preferences against behavioral and self-report criteria to assess their validity and generalizability. Each stage is described in detail below.

---

## 3.2 LLM Elicitation Pipeline

### 3.2.1 Pipeline Architecture

The elicitation pipeline is designed to map sequences of observed editing actions to natural-language representations of the latent preferences that may explain them. The pipeline operates in three sequential steps: context assembly, preference induction, and preference refinement.

In the *context assembly* step, raw editing events are transformed into structured narrative summaries. Each summary captures the textual state before and after an edit, the edit operation type (insertion, deletion, substitution, or reorder), and any available metadata such as document genre, collaboration role, and elapsed time since the prior edit. These summaries serve as the primary input to the LLM and are constructed to be self-contained, ensuring that the model's inferences are grounded in observable evidence rather than external assumptions about user intent.

In the *preference induction* step, the assembled context is passed to the LLM under a structured prompt that instructs the model to hypothesize the underlying preference most parsimoniously consistent with the observed edits. Crucially, the prompt frames this task as abductive inference: the model is instructed not to describe *what* the user did, but to articulate *why* a writer with consistent underlying preferences would have done it. Prompt design followed an iterative refinement protocol described in Section 3.2.2.

In the *preference refinement* step, candidate preferences produced in the induction step are passed through a secondary LLM call that checks for internal consistency, redundancy with previously elicited preferences for the same user, and appropriate level of specificity. Preferences that are too behavioral (e.g., "the user deletes adjectives") are escalated to a higher level of abstraction (e.g., "the user prefers nominal conciseness over expressive modification"), while preferences that are too abstract to generate testable predictions are decomposed into more grounded sub-claims.

### 3.2.2 Prompt Design and Iteration

Prompt design followed a three-phase iterative protocol. In Phase 1, an initial prompt set was drafted based on prior work on chain-of-thought elicitation and persona modeling. In Phase 2, a panel of three domain experts — two professional editors and one computational linguist — evaluated prompt outputs against a held-out set of annotated editing sessions and provided structured feedback on whether elicited preferences were (a) plausible, (b) non-obvious from surface behavior alone, and (c) specific enough to make falsifiable predictions. Prompts were revised iteratively until inter-rater agreement on plausibility and non-obviousness reached a Cohen's κ ≥ 0.70 across two consecutive evaluation rounds. In Phase 3, the finalized prompt set was frozen prior to all subsequent data collection to prevent leakage between pipeline development and evaluation.

The LLM used throughout the elicitation pipeline is [*model identifier*], accessed via [*API or local deployment*] with temperature set to 0.3 to balance output diversity with consistency. All model calls are logged and made available as supplementary material to support reproducibility.

---

## 3.3 Behavioral Data Collection and Annotation

### 3.3.1 Participant Recruitment and Study Protocol

Participants were recruited from [*population description*] using [*recruitment method*]. Inclusion criteria required that participants regularly engage in collaborative writing — defined as co-authoring or substantively editing documents with at least one other contributor — at a frequency of no fewer than three sessions per week. Participants were excluded if they had professional training in linguistics or natural language processing, as such training may systematically alter meta-awareness of writing preferences in ways that confound the implicit/explicit distinction central to this study.

Each participant completed [*N*] writing sessions within the study platform, a custom-built collaborative editor instrumented to capture fine-grained editing events at the keystroke and operation level. Sessions were not constrained to particular topics or genres; participants were instead asked to work on documents representative of their ordinary writing practice. This ecological validity design choice prioritizes the naturalness and representativeness of behavioral traces over experimental control of content.

### 3.3.2 Event Logging and Data Structure

The instrumented editor logs each editing event as a structured record containing: a unique event identifier, a session and user identifier, the operation type, the character-level diff, the document position, a Unix timestamp, and the document's collaborative context state (e.g., whether the passage being edited was authored by the participant or a co-author). All logs are stored in a normalized relational schema. Personally identifiable content is removed through a pseudonymization procedure applied prior to any analysis; document content is replaced with content-preserving paraphrases generated under strict researcher review when examples are used in annotations or qualitative analysis.

### 3.3.3 Behavioral Annotation

Editing sessions are segmented into *annotation units* defined as contiguous sequences of editing operations on a single coherent passage, bounded by pauses exceeding a threshold of [*T* seconds] or by a change in the target passage. Each annotation unit is independently labeled by two trained annotators using a structured codebook. The codebook distinguishes between *surface behavioral patterns* (e.g., consistent reduction of sentence length, systematic removal of hedging language) and *candidate preference signals* — annotation units in which the behavioral pattern is sufficiently consistent and sufficiently non-trivially explainable by local context alone to constitute evidence of a latent preference. Inter-annotator agreement is computed at the annotation-unit level using Cohen's κ; units with disagreements are resolved through adjudication by a third annotator. The resulting annotated corpus forms the ground-truth dataset against which elicited preferences are evaluated.

---

## 3.4 Preference Evaluation

### 3.4.1 Evaluation Framework

We evaluate elicited preferences along three dimensions: *behavioral consistency*, *predictive validity*, and *user recognizability*. These dimensions are designed to triangulate validity from complementary directions — behavioral consistency tests whether elicited preferences are coherent with past behavior, predictive validity tests whether they generalize to new behavior, and user recognizability tests whether they resonate with users' own, more deliberate self-understanding.

### 3.4.2 Behavioral Consistency

For each elicited preference *p* associated with user *u*, we compute a *consistency score* that measures the proportion of annotation units in *u*'s historical session data that are compatible with *p* as operationalized by a formal compatibility criterion. The compatibility criterion is derived by prompting the LLM to generate a set of behavioral predictions from *p* — specific editing patterns that a writer holding *p* would be expected to exhibit — and then checking each annotation unit against those predictions using a rule-based matching procedure. A preference is considered behaviorally consistent if its consistency score exceeds a threshold of [*τ*], calibrated against the annotated ground-truth corpus.

### 3.4.3 Predictive Validity

To assess predictive validity, we hold out the final [*k*] sessions of each participant's data and evaluate whether preferences elicited from earlier sessions predict editing behavior in the held-out sessions at rates exceeding a null model. The null model is defined as a frequency-matched baseline that predicts editing operations according to the marginal distribution of operation types in the participant's training data, without reference to any preference representation. Predictive validity is quantified as the improvement in prediction accuracy over the null model, reported as [*metric, e.g., F1 or log-likelihood ratio*], with significance assessed using a permutation test at α = 0.05.

### 3.4.4 User Recognizability

User recognizability is assessed through a structured interview protocol administered after all writing sessions are complete. Each participant is presented with a randomized set of preference statements drawn from three sources in equal proportion: preferences elicited by the pipeline for that participant (*true positives*), preferences elicited for other participants (*foils*), and surface-level behavioral descriptions that do not rise to the level of latent preference (*behavioral controls*). Participants rate each statement on a five-point Likert scale from "does not describe me at all" to "describes me very accurately." A preference is considered recognizable if it receives a mean rating significantly higher than both foils and behavioral controls, assessed using a paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test. Recognizability scores are reported both per-preference and aggregated across participants.

### 3.4.5 Composite Validity Assessment

A preference is considered *fully validated* if it meets the behavioral consistency threshold, demonstrates statistically significant predictive validity, and achieves recognizability ratings significantly above the foil and control baselines. Preferences meeting only a subset of these criteria are reported with partial-validity designations to support nuanced interpretation. The proportion of fully validated preferences across participants, and the distribution of partial-validity patterns, are reported as primary outcome measures in Section 4.

</draft>

There's a lot here that I genuinely like — the terminology is tight, the three-component structure is sensible, and the annotation protocol in 3.3.3 is particularly well-specified. But reading through it, I keep feeling like I'm being handed a manual rather than being walked through an argument. Take 3.2.1 for example: it opens immediately with what the pipeline does, but I don't yet know *why* we need a three-step pipeline rather than a simpler approach. By the time I get to the preference refinement step, I've lost the thread of why any of this is the right design choice. The same thing happens at the transitions between subsections — they feel like they're in sequence, but not like each one *requires* the previous one to have happened. Could you revisit the structure so that each paragraph earns its place by connecting to what came before it, rather than just following it?

# 3. Methodology

## 3.1 Overview

This section describes the three-component methodology employed to surface and validate implicit user preferences in collaborative writing systems. We define an *implicit preference* as a latent disposition that consistently and systematically influences a user's editing behavior but that the user does not voluntarily or reliably articulate when prompted directly. This definition creates an immediate methodological challenge: because implicit preferences are not directly observable, they cannot be collected through self-report alone, yet any preference representation we derive from behavior must ultimately be interpretable and meaningful to the user who holds it. Our methodology is structured around this tension. We first describe how we design an LLM elicitation pipeline capable of generating natural-language preference representations from behavioral traces (Section 3.2); we then describe how we collect and annotate the behavioral data those representations must explain (Section 3.3); and finally we describe how we evaluate whether the resulting preferences are coherent with observed behavior, generalize to new behavior, and resonate with users themselves (Section 3.4). The logic binding these three components is cumulative: the elicitation pipeline determines what kind of behavioral signal it requires, the data collection protocol is designed to produce that signal in a form amenable to annotation, and the evaluation criteria are derived from the specific validity claims the elicitation procedure makes.

---

## 3.2 LLM Elicitation Pipeline

### 3.2.1 Pipeline Architecture

The central design problem for any elicitation approach is the gap between what behavior shows and what preferences mean. A user who consistently shortens sentences, removes hedging language, and restructures passive constructions is exhibiting a pattern — but the pattern underdetermines the preference. It might reflect a concern for clarity, an aesthetic aversion to indirectness, a sensitivity to a particular audience, or some combination of these. A system that simply catalogs behavioral regularities has not elicited a preference; it has described a habit. Moving from description to interpretation requires inference, and that inference must be constrained enough to be consistent and general enough to generate predictions about new behavior. These twin requirements motivate the three-step pipeline described here.

The pipeline maps sequences of observed editing actions to natural-language representations of the latent preferences that most parsimoniously explain them. It operates in three sequential steps — context assembly, preference induction, and preference refinement — each of which addresses a distinct failure mode of the naive approach.

In the *context assembly* step, raw editing events are transformed into structured narrative summaries that give the model enough grounding to reason about intent rather than operation. Each summary captures the textual state before and after an edit, the edit operation type (insertion, deletion, substitution, or reorder), and available metadata including document genre, collaboration role, and elapsed time since the prior edit. Without this assembly step, the LLM would receive decontextualized diffs — inputs that invite pattern-matching rather than interpretive inference. The assembly step is therefore not preprocessing in the incidental sense; it is the mechanism by which the elicitation task is made tractable.

In the *preference induction* step, the assembled context is passed to the LLM under a structured prompt that instructs the model to hypothesize the underlying preference most parsimoniously consistent with the observed edits. The prompt frames this as abductive inference: the model is instructed not to describe *what* the user did, but to articulate *why* a writer with consistent underlying preferences would have done it. This framing matters because it enforces a level of abstraction that purely descriptive prompting does not. A model asked to describe behavior will describe behavior; a model asked to explain behavior under an assumption of rational preference consistency is constrained to produce representations that can, in principle, generalize. The prompt design protocol through which this framing was operationalized and validated is described in Section 3.2.2.

The induction step, however, introduces its own failure mode: without further constraint, the LLM produces candidate preferences that vary in their level of abstraction, may overlap with or contradict one another across sessions, and occasionally collapse back into behavioral description despite prompt instructions. The *preference refinement* step exists to correct for this. Candidate preferences produced during induction are passed through a secondary LLM call that checks for internal consistency, redundancy with previously elicited preferences for the same user, and appropriate specificity. Preferences that remain too behavioral (e.g., "the user deletes adjectives") are escalated to a higher level of abstraction (e.g., "the user prefers nominal conciseness over expressive modification"), while preferences that are too abstract to generate testable predictions are decomposed into more grounded sub-claims. The refinement step is what allows the pipeline's outputs to serve as inputs to the evaluation framework in Section 3.4: without it, the elicited preferences would not be consistent enough to assess systematically.

### 3.2.2 Prompt Design and Iteration

The induction step's validity depends directly on whether the prompts reliably elicit interpretive, non-redundant, appropriately abstract preferences rather than behavioral redescriptions. Establishing this required an iterative prompt development protocol conducted prior to and entirely separate from behavioral data collection.

Prompt design proceeded in three phases. In Phase 1, an initial prompt set was drafted based on prior work on chain-of-thought elicitation and persona modeling. These prompts established the abductive framing described above but had not yet been tested against realistic editing data. In Phase 2, a panel of three domain experts — two professional editors and one computational linguist — evaluated prompt outputs against a held-out set of annotated editing sessions and provided structured feedback on whether elicited preferences were (a) plausible given the observed behavior, (b) non-obvious from surface patterns alone, and (c) specific enough to make falsifiable predictions about future editing. The criterion of non-obviousness is particularly important: a preference that any reader could infer directly from the behavioral description adds nothing over behavioral annotation and would not justify the elicitation procedure. Prompts were revised iteratively until inter-rater agreement on plausibility and non-obviousness reached Cohen's κ ≥ 0.70 across two consecutive evaluation rounds. In Phase 3, the finalized prompt set was frozen prior to all subsequent data collection to prevent any leakage between pipeline development and evaluation.

The LLM used throughout the elicitation pipeline is [*model identifier*], accessed via [*API or local deployment*] with temperature set to 0.3 to balance output diversity with consistency. All model calls are logged and made available as supplementary material to support reproducibility.

---

## 3.3 Behavioral Data Collection and Annotation

### 3.3.1 Participant Recruitment and Study Protocol

The elicitation pipeline described in Section 3.2 requires behavioral traces that are both ecologically valid — representing the kinds of editing decisions through which implicit preferences actually manifest — and rich enough to support the abductive inference the induction step performs. Thin or atypical behavioral data would defeat the pipeline regardless of prompt quality. The data collection protocol is therefore designed to maximize the naturalness and density of editing behavior, at some cost to experimental control of content.

Participants were recruited from [*population description*] using [*recruitment method*]. Inclusion criteria required that participants regularly engage in collaborative writing — defined as co-authoring or substantively editing documents with at least one other contributor — at a frequency of no fewer than three sessions per week. Participants were excluded if they had professional training in linguistics or natural language processing, as such training may systematically heighten meta-awareness of writing preferences in ways that blur the implicit/explicit distinction the study is designed to investigate.

Each participant completed [*N*] writing sessions within the study platform, a custom-built collaborative editor instrumented to capture fine-grained editing events at the keystroke and operation level. Sessions were not constrained to particular topics or genres; participants were instead asked to work on documents representative of their ordinary writing practice. This choice sacrifices content comparability across participants but preserves the authenticity of the behavioral signal — implicit preferences are most likely to manifest when writers are not consciously adjusting to an unfamiliar task.

### 3.3.2 Event Logging and Data Structure

Raw editing events are the substrate from which the context assembly step constructs its narrative summaries, and the quality of that substrate depends on the granularity and consistency of the logging scheme. The instrumented editor logs each editing event as a structured record containing: a unique event identifier, a session and user identifier, the operation type, the character-level diff, the document position, a Unix timestamp, and the document's collaborative context state (e.g., whether the passage being edited was authored by the participant or a co-author). All logs are stored in a normalized relational schema. The collaborative context state field is particularly consequential: implicit preferences may manifest differently when a writer is editing their own prose versus a co-author's, and conflating these two cases would introduce noise into both the elicitation and evaluation stages.

Personally identifiable content is removed through a pseudonymization procedure applied prior to any analysis; document content is replaced with content-preserving paraphrases generated under strict researcher review when examples appear in annotations or qualitative analysis.

### 3.3.3 Behavioral Annotation

The logged event data must be annotated before it can serve either as input to the elicitation pipeline or as ground truth for evaluation. This requires a unit of analysis at an intermediate level of granularity: individual keystrokes are too fine-grained to carry interpretive weight, while full sessions are too coarse to isolate preference-relevant behavior from incidental variation. We define *annotation units* as contiguous sequences of editing operations on a single coherent passage, bounded by pauses exceeding a threshold of [*T* seconds] or by a change in the target passage. This segmentation preserves the local coherence of editing decisions while keeping units small enough for annotators to reason about intent.

Each annotation unit is independently labeled by two trained annotators using a structured codebook. The codebook distinguishes between *surface behavioral patterns* — regularities that are directly readable from the edit sequence, such as consistent sentence-length reduction — and *candidate preference signals*: annotation units in which the behavioral pattern is sufficiently consistent and sufficiently non-trivially explainable by local context alone to constitute evidence of a latent preference. This distinction maps directly onto the non-obviousness criterion used in prompt evaluation (Section 3.2.2), ensuring that the annotation scheme and the elicitation procedure are calibrated against the same underlying construct.

Inter-annotator agreement is computed at the annotation-unit level using Cohen's κ; units with disagreements are resolved through adjudication by a third annotator. The resulting annotated corpus serves two roles in subsequent analysis: it provides the behavioral input to the elicitation pipeline for participants in the development set, and it provides the ground-truth labels against which elicited preferences are evaluated for all participants.

---

## 3.4 Preference Evaluation

### 3.4.1 Evaluation Framework

An elicited preference representation is only as useful as it is valid, and validity here has an irreducibly multi-dimensional character. A preference that coheres with past behavior but fails to predict future behavior may reflect overfitting to the elicitation context rather than a genuine latent disposition. A preference that predicts behavior but that the user finds unrecognizable may be capturing a real regularity at the wrong level of description — one that is statistically coherent but not meaningfully connected to the writer's own understanding of their practice. A preference that users readily recognize but that does not predict behavior may reflect socially desirable self-presentation rather than implicit disposition. No single criterion is sufficient; the three must be assessed together. We therefore evaluate elicited preferences along three dimensions — *behavioral consistency*, *predictive validity*, and *user recognizability* — treating full validity as the conjunction of all three.

### 3.4.2 Behavioral Consistency

Behavioral consistency asks whether an elicited preference coheres with the historical editing data from which it was derived. For each elicited preference *p* associated with user *u*, we compute a *consistency score* measuring the proportion of annotation units in *u*'s historical session data that are compatible with *p* as operationalized by a formal compatibility criterion. The compatibility criterion is derived by prompting the LLM to generate a set of behavioral predictions from *p* — specific editing patterns that a writer holding *p* would be expected to exhibit — and then checking each annotation unit against those predictions using a rule-based matching procedure. Consistency scoring thus closes a loop with the elicitation pipeline: the same LLM that produced the preference is used to specify what that preference predicts, making the consistency assessment sensitive to the internal logic of the elicited representation rather than to an externally imposed operationalization. A preference is considered behaviorally consistent if its consistency score exceeds a threshold of [*τ*], calibrated against the annotated ground-truth corpus.

### 3.4.3 Predictive Validity

Behavioral consistency is a necessary condition for validity but not a sufficient one, since a preference representation could be constructed post hoc to fit any behavioral trace without generalizing beyond it. Predictive validity assesses whether elicited preferences actually generalize. To measure this, we hold out the final [*k*] sessions of each participant's data and evaluate whether preferences elicited from earlier sessions predict editing behavior in the held-out sessions at rates exceeding a null model. The null model is defined as a frequency-matched baseline that predicts editing operations according to the marginal distribution of operation types in the participant's training data, without reference to any preference representation. This null model is deliberately weak: it represents the prediction accuracy achievable with no understanding of the participant's preferences whatsoever, and improvement over it constitutes a lower bound on the preference representations' generative usefulness. Predictive validity is quantified as improvement in prediction accuracy over the null model, reported as [*metric*], with significance assessed using a permutation test at α = 0.05.

### 3.4.4 User Recognizability

Even a preference that is behaviorally consistent and predictively valid may fail to constitute a useful representation of an implicit preference if it does not connect in any way to the user's own self-understanding. Recognizability does not require that users have previously articulated the preference — if they had, it would not be implicit — but it does require that, when presented with the elicited representation, users find it accurate rather than foreign. This criterion guards against the failure mode of a pipeline that produces behaviorally coherent but phenomenologically opaque representations.

Recognizability is assessed through a structured interview protocol administered after all writing sessions are complete. Each participant is presented with a randomized set of preference statements drawn from three sources in equal proportion: preferences elicited by the pipeline for that participant (*target preferences*), preferences elicited for other participants (*foils*), and surface-level behavioral descriptions that do not rise to the level of latent preference (*behavioral controls*). The inclusion of both foils and behavioral controls is deliberate: foils test whether participants endorse preferences specifically because they are their own, while behavioral controls test whether the elicited preference adds interpretive value over simple behavioral description — the same non-obviousness criterion that governed prompt development and annotation. Participants rate each statement on a five-point Likert scale from "does not describe me at all" to "describes me very accurately." A preference is considered recognizable if it receives a mean rating significantly higher than both foils and behavioral controls, assessed using a paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test.

### 3.4.5 Composite Validity Assessment

A preference is considered *fully validated* if it meets the behavioral consistency threshold, demonstrates statistically significant predictive validity, and achieves recognizability ratings significantly above both the foil and behavioral control baselines. Preferences meeting only a subset of these criteria are reported with partial-validity designations. The pattern of partial validity is itself analytically informative: preferences that are behaviorally consistent and predictively valid but not recognizable point to a representational mismatch between the pipeline's abstraction level and the user's vocabulary for self-description; preferences that are recognizable but not predictively valid may indicate that users are endorsing aspirational rather than actual dispositions. The proportion of fully validated preferences across participants, and the distribution of partial-validity patterns, are reported as primary outcome measures in Section 4.

This revision is genuinely stronger — the dependency-based transitions in 3.2 are working well now, and the three-step pipeline architecture reads like an argument rather than a list. The 'failure mode' logic threading through 3.2.1 is exactly the right kind of connective tissue.

But there's something I keep noticing as I read: the whole section is written at the same register, the same level of abstraction, all the way through. And paradoxically that makes it feel slightly less confident, not more. The best methods sections I've read — the ones that feel authoritative — have one or two moments where the author steps back and just says what the key insight actually is, in plain terms. Not dumbing it down, but demonstrating they understand it well enough to say it simply. Right now the draft never does that. It explains everything at equal intensity, which means nothing stands out as the conceptual core. I'd look at the transition between the induction and refinement steps, or the moment in 3.4.1 where you're setting up the three-dimensional validity framework — somewhere in there, there's a place where a single plain-language sentence would make the whole structure click into place for the reader.

Good diagnosis. There are two moments in the draft where the argument is doing something genuinely non-obvious, and both of them are currently buried under the same even prose register as everything around them. Here's what I'd do at each location:

**At the transition between induction and refinement in 3.2.1**, after the sentence ending "...constrained to produce representations that can, in principle, generalize," add:

> The problem is that this constraint is imperfect in practice: left to induction alone, the pipeline produces preferences that are locally coherent but globally inconsistent. The refinement step exists because inference is not enough — the outputs of inference still need to be managed.

**At the opening of 3.4.1**, replace the current first sentence with:

> The core difficulty in evaluating elicited preferences is that there is no ground truth. We cannot directly observe what a user's implicit preferences actually are — if we could, elicitation would be unnecessary. Evaluation therefore cannot ask whether we recovered the right answer; it can only ask whether the answer we recovered is coherent with behavior, useful for prediction, and meaningful to the person it describes. Each of the three criteria below tests a different way the answer could be wrong.

---

The first of these is the sharper intervention. The current draft explains what refinement does at length but never says plainly why induction alone is insufficient — which means a reader can follow the logic without quite grasping the necessity. The second moment is more structural: it reframes the three-criterion validity framework as a response to an epistemic problem rather than a methodological preference, which makes the whole evaluation design feel motivated rather than chosen.

Want me to integrate both into a full revised draft, or work through them one at a time?
Could you write up a full draft for me based on what we've discussed?

Sessions were not constrained to particular topics or genres; participants were instead asked to work on documents representative of their ordinary writing practice. This choice sacrifices content comparability across participants but preserves the authenticity of the behavioral signal — implicit preferences are most likely to manifest when writers are not consciously adjusting to an unfamiliar task.

### 3.3.2 Event Logging and Data Structure

Raw editing events are the substrate from which the context assembly step constructs its narrative summaries, and the quality of that substrate depends on the granularity and consistency of the logging scheme. The instrumented editor logs each editing event as a structured record containing: a unique event identifier, a session and user identifier, the operation type, the character-level diff, the document position, a Unix timestamp, and the document's collaborative context state (e.g., whether the passage being edited was authored by the participant or a co-author). All logs are stored in a normalized relational schema. The collaborative context state field is particularly consequential: implicit preferences may manifest differently when a writer is editing their own prose versus a co-author's, and conflating these two cases would introduce noise into both the elicitation and evaluation stages.

Personally identifiable content is removed through a pseudonymization procedure applied prior to any analysis; document content is replaced with content-preserving paraphrases generated under strict researcher review when examples appear in annotations or qualitative analysis.

### 3.3.3 Behavioral Annotation

The logged event data must be annotated before it can serve either as input to the elicitation pipeline or as ground truth for evaluation. This requires a unit of analysis at an intermediate level of granularity: individual keystrokes are too fine-grained to carry interpretive weight, while full sessions are too coarse to isolate preference-relevant behavior from incidental variation. We define *annotation units* as contiguous sequences of editing operations on a single coherent passage, bounded by pauses exceeding a threshold of [*T* seconds] or by a change in the target passage. This segmentation preserves the local coherence of editing decisions while keeping units small enough for annotators to reason about intent.

Each annotation unit is independently labeled by two trained annotators using a structured codebook. The codebook distinguishes between *surface behavioral patterns* — regularities that are directly readable from the edit sequence, such as consistent sentence-length reduction — and *candidate preference signals*: annotation units in which the behavioral pattern is sufficiently consistent and sufficiently non-trivially explainable by local context alone to constitute evidence of a latent preference. This distinction maps directly onto the non-obviousness criterion used in prompt evaluation (Section 3.2.2), ensuring that the annotation scheme and the elicitation procedure are calibrated against the same underlying construct.

Inter-annotator agreement is computed at the annotation-unit level using Cohen's κ; units with disagreements are resolved through adjudication by a third annotator. The resulting annotated corpus serves two roles in subsequent analysis: it provides the behavioral input to the elicitation pipeline for participants in the development set, and it provides the ground-truth labels against which elicited preferences are evaluated for all participants.

---

## 3.4 Preference Evaluation

### 3.4.1 Evaluation Framework

The core difficulty in evaluating elicited preferences is that there is no ground truth. We cannot directly observe what a user's implicit preferences actually are — if we could, elicitation would be unnecessary. Evaluation therefore cannot ask whether we recovered the right answer; it can only ask whether the answer we recovered is coherent with behavior, useful for prediction, and meaningful to the person it describes. Each of the three criteria below tests a different way the answer could be wrong.

We evaluate elicited preferences along three dimensions: *behavioral consistency*, *predictive validity*, and *user recognizability*. A preference that coheres with past behavior but fails to predict future behavior may reflect overfitting to the elicitation context rather than a genuine latent disposition. A preference that predicts behavior but that the user finds unrecognizable may be capturing a real regularity at the wrong level of description — one that is statistically coherent but not meaningfully connected to the writer's own understanding of their practice. A preference that users readily recognize but that does not predict behavior may reflect socially desirable self-presentation rather than implicit disposition. No single criterion is sufficient; the three must be assessed together, and full validity is treated as their conjunction.

### 3.4.2 Behavioral Consistency

Behavioral consistency asks whether an elicited preference coheres with the historical editing data from which it was derived. For each elicited preference *p* associated with user *u*, we compute a *consistency score* measuring the proportion of annotation units in *u*'s historical session data that are compatible with *p* as operationalized by a formal compatibility criterion. The compatibility criterion is derived by prompting the LLM to generate a set of behavioral predictions from *p* — specific editing patterns that a writer holding *p* would be expected to exhibit — and then checking each annotation unit against those predictions using a rule-based matching procedure. Consistency scoring thus closes a loop with the elicitation pipeline: the same LLM that produced the preference is used to specify what that preference predicts, making the consistency assessment sensitive to the internal logic of the elicited representation rather than to an externally imposed operationalization. A preference is considered behaviorally consistent if its consistency score exceeds a threshold of [*τ*], calibrated against the annotated ground-truth corpus.

### 3.4.3 Predictive Validity

Behavioral consistency is a necessary condition for validity but not a sufficient one, since a preference representation could be constructed post hoc to fit any behavioral trace without generalizing beyond it. Predictive validity assesses whether elicited preferences actually generalize. To measure this, we hold out the final [*k*] sessions of each participant's data and evaluate whether preferences elicited from earlier sessions predict editing behavior in the held-out sessions at rates exceeding a null model. The null model is defined as a frequency-matched baseline that predicts editing operations according to the marginal distribution of operation types in the participant's training data, without reference to any preference representation. This null model is deliberately weak: it represents the prediction accuracy achievable with no understanding of the participant's preferences whatsoever, and improvement over it constitutes a lower bound on the preference representations' generative usefulness. Predictive validity is quantified as improvement in prediction accuracy over the null model, reported as [*metric*], with significance assessed using a permutation test at α = 0.05.

### 3.4.4 User Recognizability

Even a preference that is behaviorally consistent and predictively valid may fail to constitute a useful representation of an implicit preference if it does not connect in any way to the user's own self-understanding. Recognizability does not require that users have previously articulated the preference — if they had, it would not be implicit — but it does require that, when presented with the elicited representation, users find it accurate rather than foreign. This criterion guards against the failure mode of a pipeline that produces behaviorally coherent but phenomenologically opaque representations.

Recognizability is assessed through a structured interview protocol administered after all writing sessions are complete. Each participant is presented with a randomized set of preference statements drawn from three sources in equal proportion: preferences elicited by the pipeline for that participant (*target preferences*), preferences elicited for other participants (*foils*), and surface-level behavioral descriptions that do not rise to the level of latent preference (*behavioral controls*). The inclusion of both foils and behavioral controls is deliberate: foils test whether participants endorse preferences specifically because they are their own, while behavioral controls test whether the elicited preference adds interpretive value over simple behavioral description — the same non-obviousness criterion that governed prompt development and annotation. Participants rate each statement on a five-point Likert scale from "does not describe me at all" to "describes me very accurately." A preference is considered recognizable if it receives a mean rating significantly higher than both foils and behavioral controls, assessed using a paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test.

### 3.4.5 Composite Validity Assessment

A preference is considered *fully validated* if it meets the behavioral consistency threshold, demonstrates statistically significant predictive validity, and achieves recognizability ratings significantly above both the foil and behavioral control baselines. Preferences meeting only a subset of these criteria are reported with partial-validity designations. The pattern of partial validity is itself analytically informative: preferences that are behaviorally consistent and predictively valid but not recognizable point to a representational mismatch between the pipeline's abstraction level and the user's vocabulary for self-description; preferences that are recognizable but not predictively valid may indicate that users are endorsing aspirational rather than actual dispositions. The proportion of fully validated preferences across participants, and the distribution of partial-validity patterns, are reported as primary outcome measures in Section 4.

I have a clear sense of what we did, but I want to make sure the writing earns the reader's trust before we get into the formal definitions, rather than just dropping them into a list of metrics.

<draft>Measuring linguistic complexity is straightforward in principle and treacherous in practice. The treachery lies in conflation: most candidate metrics — mean sentence length, type-token ratio, subordination depth — capture partially overlapping variance, so treating any single measure as a proxy for complexity risks mistaking one surface feature for the underlying construct. Equally, aggregating metrics without theoretical motivation produces a composite that is statistically convenient but conceptually opaque.

We therefore approached operationalization in two stages. First, we asked which dimensions of complexity are theoretically independent — that is, which could plausibly vary in opposite directions within the same text. A speaker may produce syntactically elaborate but lexically impoverished output; a sentence may be long without being deeply embedded. This separability requirement determined our initial candidate set: syntactic complexity, operationalized as mean length of T-unit (MLT) and subordination index (SI); lexical complexity, operationalized as Guiraud's corrected type-token ratio (CTTR); and propositional density, operationalized as the ratio of propositional content words to total words following Turner and Greene's (1977) procedure. These four measures were selected not because they exhaust the construct but because they are mutually non-redundant given the texts under analysis.

Because non-redundancy is an empirical claim rather than a guarantee, we subjected the four measures to principal components analysis prior to any hypothesis testing. The goal was to determine whether the candidate measures fractionate into distinct dimensions or collapse toward a single factor — a result that would force a different analytic strategy. Components with eigenvalues exceeding 1.0 were retained; loadings below |.40| were treated as negligible. This step matters: a single-factor solution would mean that our four operationalizations are measuring one thing under four names, in which case using them as separate dependent variables would inflate Type I error without adding explanatory resolution.

The point, stated plainly, is that the structure of the dependent variable cannot be assumed — it must be established before any comparisons between conditions are meaningful.</draft>

<draft>The central question motivating this Discussion is not whether syntactic complexity affects reading comprehension — that relationship is well established — but whether prior operationalizations have measured it accurately enough to support the theoretical claims built upon them. Our three-dimensional operationalization — distinguishing hierarchical depth, phrasal elaboration, and cross-clausal dependency — was designed precisely to address this failure mode. Where prior approaches collapsed these into one score, ours keeps them separable, allowing us to ask which dimension drives comprehension costs under which conditions. That conflation matters because it has produced an inconsistent literature. Studies reporting null or contradictory effects of syntactic complexity on comprehension are disproportionately those employing single-metric designs, a pattern consistent with construct underspecification rather than a genuine absence of effect (cf. McNamara & Kintsch, 1996; Just & Carpenter, 1992). Put simply: the field has not been finding weak effects because the effects are weak — it has been finding weak effects because the measurement was coarse. Our three-dimensional operationalization — distinguishing hierarchical depth, phrasal elaboration, and cross-clausal dependency — was designed precisely to address this failure mode. Where prior approaches collapsed these into one score, ours keeps them separable, allowing us to ask which dimension drives comprehension costs under which conditions. The results reported above bear out that design decision: the three dimensions predicted unique variance and did so in theoretically coherent, dimension-specific patterns that a composite score would have suppressed entirely.</draft>

I think we're in good shape with the Discussion opening. Now I need to draft the Methods section — specifically the subsection on how we operationalized syntactic complexity. Can you write something that covers our annotation pipeline and the decisions we made about which complexity metrics to include?

<draft>Operationalizing syntactic complexity required resolving a prior question: what exactly are we measuring, and why does the choice matter? Surface-level proxies such as mean sentence length or type-token ratio are available off the shelf, but they conflate distinct structural phenomena — a long sentence built from coordinate clauses makes different cognitive demands than one built from center-embedded relative clauses — and using them interchangeably would undermine the precision our central argument requires. We therefore define syntactic complexity as the degree of hierarchical embedding and dependency distance within a sentence's constituent structure, following the framework established by Gibson (1998) and operationalized in subsequent corpus work by Gildea and Jurafsky (2002).

Because no single metric captures this construct fully, the annotation pipeline integrates three complementary measures: mean dependency distance (MDD), the ratio of non-projective dependencies, and the depth of the maximum embedded clause per sentence. Each addresses a failure mode the others leave visible. MDD captures the average integration cost across all dependency arcs but is insensitive to the distribution of that cost; non-projectivity targets crossing dependencies that impose localized working-memory spikes even when MDD is modest; and maximum embedding depth identifies the structural worst case that aggregate metrics smooth over. Together, these three metrics triangulate syntactic complexity in a way no individual measure could.

The annotation itself proceeded in two stages. In the first stage, we parsed all sentences using the Stanford Neural Dependency Parser (Manning et al., 2014) with the Universal Dependencies schema, yielding typed dependency trees from which MDD and non-projectivity counts were extracted automatically. Inter-rater reliability for this stage was not applicable, as the extraction was fully deterministic given the parse. The second stage involved manual verification of maximum embedding depth for a stratified random sample of 300 sentences (approximately 15% of the corpus), with two annotators independently coding clause boundaries. Cohen's κ was 0.81, indicating strong agreement and supporting the reliability of the automated depth estimates across the full corpus. Sentences where the two coders disagreed were adjudicated by a third annotator, and the adjudicated labels were used in all subsequent analyses.

The practical consequence of this three-metric design is that complexity enters our models not as a single covariate but as a structured profile. That is the point: sentences with similar aggregate scores can differ sharply in the source of their difficulty, and our design preserves that distinction rather than collapsing it.</draft>

rubric.name: Argumentative Flow
rubric.description: Each paragraph and subsection earns its place through logical dependency, not mere sequence. Paragraphs open by establishing their role in the argument before presenting content — opening like a reason, not like a finding. The section builds toward its most important claim so the reader senses a destination early, and dense paragraphs synthesize their collective findings before transitioning. Well-placed paragraphs should feel inevitable rather than appended.

dimensions.label: Each paragraph's opening connects explicitly to the preceding paragraph's conclusion, claim, or identified gap — the paragraph feels inevitable rather than merely next
Each paragraph establishes the question, role, or stakes it will address before presenting findings, descriptions, or data — it opens like a reason, not like a finding
Sections and subsections are logically dependent on one another, with transitions expressing necessity (because, therefore, this requires) rather than sequence (next, additionally)
The section builds toward its most important claim, with the reader able to sense the destination early rather than encountering it as a surprise
Dense paragraphs presenting multiple findings include a synthesis beat that names what the findings collectively establish before transitioning to the next paragraph

rubric.name: Design Motivation and Justification
rubric.description: Methodological choices, analytical approaches, and evaluative frameworks are justified before they are described. The reader encounters the problem, failure mode, or question that necessitates a component before learning what that component does or what it found. Formal definitions and metrics earn the reader's trust through conceptual grounding rather than arriving as lists.

dimensions.label: Each major component is preceded by the problem, failure mode, or question it addresses
Naive or alternative approaches are named explicitly to clarify why the chosen approach is necessary
Justifications are specific to the design's internal logic, not generic appeals to best practice or convention
Later components are motivated by the outputs, limitations, or requirements established by earlier components
The analytical approach or testing strategy is briefly established before findings are presented

rubric.name: Terminological Precision and Consistency
rubric.description: Key constructs are formally defined and then used with strict consistency. The same concept is referred to with the same term throughout — no unnecessary synonymic variation — and technical terms are clearly distinguished from everyday usage.

dimensions.label: Key constructs are formally defined at their first appearance
The same concept is referred to with the same term throughout the text without unnecessary variation
Technical terms are clearly distinguished from everyday language through formatting, definition, or explicit framing
Terms introduced in one section are reused consistently when the same construct reappears later

rubric.name: Stakes and Implications
rubric.description: The text articulates what findings and design choices actually mean — not just what was found, but what it buys us, what it changes, and why the reader should care. Implications are woven throughout the section rather than saved for the end, and major findings reframe earlier claims to reveal their shifted meaning.

dimensions.label: Key findings or design choices are accompanied by explicit statements of what they mean or imply
At least one moment draws out practical or theoretical consequences beyond the immediate result
Major findings reframe earlier results, showing how their significance has shifted in light of the new evidence
The reader can identify which findings or claims the author considers most consequential and why
Implications and 'why this matters' beats are woven throughout the section rather than concentrated at the end

rubric.name: Formal Rigor and Tonal Calibration
rubric.description: Procedures, protocols, and evaluative criteria are described with enough detail to be reproducible and assessable. Statistical reporting is clean and precise, claims about prior work are grounded with citations that function as anchors for authority rather than mere convention, and word choices maintain confidence without editorializing — letting the evidence carry the weight rather than the rhetoric.

dimensions.label: Procedures and protocols are described with sufficient detail that another researcher could replicate them
Claims about methodological properties (validity, reliability, rigor) are operationalized with specific criteria, not just asserted
Statistical or evaluative criteria include precise specifications (thresholds, metrics, significance levels)
Claims about the broader literature or prior work are anchored with citations that function as evidence for authority, not just convention — unanchored claims read as asserted rather than established
Word choices convey confidence without editorializing or triumphalism — reporting rather than celebrating — so that evidence carries the weight (e.g., 'bear out' over 'vindicate')

rubric.name: Strategic Register Variation
rubric.description: The prose is not written at a single uniform register throughout. At structurally pivotal moments — where the conceptual core lives — the author steps back and states the key insight in plain, direct language that demonstrates confident mastery. These moments are brief, rare, unhedged, and make the surrounding formal analysis feel more rigorous, not less.

dimensions.label: Key conceptual insights are stated in plain, direct language that contrasts with the surrounding formal register
The most important ideas receive rhetorical emphasis through simplicity, not additional complexity
Register shifts occur at structurally pivotal moments (major transitions, framework introductions, conceptual cores) rather than at arbitrary points
Plain-language moments convey authority and mastery — demonstrating the author understands the idea well enough to say it simply — without tipping into casualness
Plain-language pivots are used sparingly (once or twice per section) so that each one carries weight

rubric.writing_type: academic research papers (methods, results, and discussion sections)
rubric.user_goals_summary: The user writes academic prose that reads as an argument rather than a report. Every paragraph, subsection, and design choice must earn its place through logical dependency, and the reader should feel pulled toward the most important claim rather than informed of findings in sequence. The prose maintains formal rigor throughout, punctuated by rare, precisely placed moments of plain-language synthesis that signal mastery.
rubric.coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: every paragraph must open by telling the reader why they are in that paragraph before content arrives — the 'opens like a reason, not a finding' principle is the single most consistent demand. Preferred trade-off: the user will sacrifice content comparability and even some formal completeness for argumentative coherence — they would rather a section do less but have every piece earn its place than cover more ground sequentially. When providing feedback, the user identifies problems with surgical precision and expects targeted fixes rather than wholesale rewrites; they respond best to options that name exactly where the intervention goes and what structural job it performs.
